topic: A person discovers their child has the same teacher they had
raw_output:
[story 1]

The envelope sat on the kitchen counter, crisp and white, a stark insult to my carefully ordered life. Inside was a letter from the school administration, detailing the enrollment of my youngest, Leo, into Mrs. Albright's third-grade class. My jaw tightened as I read the name. Mrs. Albright. The woman whose teaching style I had always found grating—too effusive, too focused on 'emotional development' when all I wanted was measurable academic progress. A slow, cold wave washed over me. I picked up the letter, the paper crinkling under my thumb, and walked over to the window, staring out at the perfectly manicured lawn, feeling a profound sense of weary dismissal.

I remember thinking, *of course*. It was as if the universe had a sick sense of humor, meticulously arranging my life so that the most irritating variables would inevitably collide. I didn't bother to tear the letter; I simply folded it once, precisely, and placed it in a drawer, the sound of the latch clicking echoing the finality of my judgment. The thought of Leo being subjected to that particular brand of pedagogy made my stomach clench with a sour, unpleasant feeling.

[story 2]

I stared at the photograph on the mantelpiece. It was Leo, beaming, clutching a brightly colored construction paper star. And next to him, in the background of the picture, was Mrs. Albright. That woman. The one whose voice always seemed artificially bright, whose enthusiasm felt manufactured. A tight knot formed in my chest, a feeling like being constricted by cold wire. I let out a short, humorless puff of air through my nose. It was absurd. Utterly, ridiculously absurd.

"Well, isn't that just perfect," I muttered to myself, my voice flat, devoid of any warmth. I walked into the living room, pacing three short steps before stopping abruptly. I needed to process this intrusion of familiarity. It felt like being forced to watch a poorly rehearsed play. I picked up my phone, intending to call the school, but hesitated. What was the point? To dissect the similarities? It was beneath me.

[story 3]

The notification popped up on my tablet: enrollment confirmation. Leo. Mrs. Albright. The realization hit me with a dull thud, settling heavily in my gut. I leaned back in my chair, the leather groaning in protest beneath me. My hands clenched into fists on the armrests, the knuckles turning white. I didn't move to check the details immediately. Instead, I focused on the sheer inconvenience of it all. It was a predictable pattern, a cruel little loop I hadn't managed to escape.

"Seriously?" I whispered, the sound brittle in the quiet room. The air felt thick, heavy with irritation. I stood up, moving towards the door, my posture rigid. There was no excitement, no shock—just a deep, ingrained sense of annoyance that someone had managed to engineer this particular alignment of circumstances. I didn't want to confront anyone; the situation itself was enough to warrant a severe tightening around my features. I simply needed distance from the inescapable symmetry.
